title: Explore BGCs
text:
Use the three tabs at the top:

Advanced Search: Combine classes, Pfams, biome words and other filters.
Sequence Search: Paste a nucleotide or protein sequence to look for similar predicted regions.
Chemical Structure Search: Draw a molecule or paste a SMILES string to find clusters linked to related chemistry.

After you click Apply / Search the request is processed in the background. If results are not ready you will briefly see a waiting screen; it updates automatically—no need to refresh manually.

title: Explore BGCs
text:
Use the three tabs at the top:

Advanced Search: Combine classes, Pfams, biome words and other filters.
Sequence Search: Paste a nucleotide or protein sequence to look for similar predicted regions.
Chemical Structure Search: Draw a molecule or paste a SMILES string to find clusters linked to related chemistry.

After you click Apply / Search the request is processed in the background. If results are not ready you will briefly see a waiting screen; it updates automatically—no need to refresh manually.

title: Explore BGCs
text:
Advanced search
The Advanced Search form allows you to perform fine-grained queries to explore the BGC Data Portal. Below is a detailed explanation of each search field and examples of complex search queries to help you utilise this feature effectively.

Search fields (Advanced tab)

BGC Class Name:
Enter one or more BGC class names (i.e., alkaloid, nrp, other, polyketide, ripp, saccharide, or terpene) separated by spaces or commas. The search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the specified classes (using an OR logic).
MGYB:
Specify a particular MGYB (BGC accession of portal) to directly retrieve data associated with that BGC.
Assembly Accession:
Input an ENA assembly accession to find predicted BGCs within that specific assembly.
Contig MGYC:
Provide a specific MGYC (contig accession) to locate BGCs within that contig.
Biome:
Enter terms related to the biome lineage (e.g., sediment, hydrothermal vent) to filter BGCs that originate from assemblies associated with those biomes.
BGC Completeness:
Select from the following options to refine your search based on the BGCs’ contig boundaries:

Full-Length BGC: The BGC is entirely within the contig, meaning both start and end positions are within the boundaries of the contig, indicating a complete prediction.
Single: Partial BGC (Single edge). The BGC extends to one end of the contig, suggesting that the prediction may be truncated at one side because the contig ends there.
Double: Partial BGC (Double edges). The BGC spans the entire length of the contig, indicating that the prediction is likely truncated on both sides, with both ends of the BGC coinciding with the ends of the contig.

Pfam: Enter one or more domain accessions (e.g., PF00109 PF00501). Use OR to be inclusive; AND to demand all domains together.
Pfam strategy:
The Pfam strategy field determines how the search will interpret multiple Pfam entries. You have two options:

OR logic: When you select the OR strategy, the search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the Pfams you specified. This is useful when you are interested in BGCs that have any of a list of potential Pfams, allowing for a broader search. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and select OR, the search will return BGCs that contain either PF00109, PF00501, or both.
AND logic: Selecting the AND strategy will narrow the search results to BGCs that must contain all the Pfams you listed. This is ideal when you are looking for BGCs with a specific combination of Pfams. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and choose AND, only those BGCs that contain both PF00109 and PF00501 will be returned.

BGC Detectors: Filter BGCs detected by the supported detectors:

antiSMASH
GECCO
SanntiS

title: Explore BGCs
text:
Results views
Results appear with three tabs plus a summary panel on the left. Click rows, card titles or points in the plot to open full BGC details in a new tab.


Table view
Sortable table; click a column header to flip the order. Your chosen sort carries through to the TSV download.


Results summary

Number of Regions

Number of studies where regions matching the criteria where found
Number of assemblies where regions matching the criteria where found
Class Distribution. Note that for regions with more than one class predicted only one will be counted.



Table columns

MGYB: The BGC accession (or aggregated accession) for the resulting region.
Assembly: The ENA assembly accession number where the BGC was predicted.
MGYC: The accession of the contig containing the BGC.
Start: The start coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
End: The end coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
Detectors: Lists the BGC detectors that contributed to the aggregated prediction.
Classes: The predicted BGC classes within the aggregated region.
Details: Link to the BGC details page.



Card view
One result per card—helpful for quickly scanning a modest number of hits or inspecting rendered structures when present.


UMAP view
2‑D map of a sampled set of clusters (for large searches). Colour = predicted class; larger points = important references (e.g. MiBIG). Hover for details; click to open the BGC page. A note appears if only a subset is plotted.


Downloading search results
Click “Download results TSV” to save the current hit list.


Practical tips

Start broad (one class + a biome word) then refine.
Combine Pfam AND logic sparingly—over‑restricting often yields zero hits.
Large result sets: prioritise using the UMAP to spot outliers or class clusters before downloading.

title: Home Page
text: The Home Page serves as the primary navigation hub for the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters. This page provides quick access to the main functionalities of the portal, allowing users to search for BGCs, access API documentation, find user guides, and learn more about the project.

title: Home Page
text:
Keyword search
At the top of every page, you will find the Keyword Search bar. This feature allows you to quickly perform a basic search across the portal data and documents using specific keywords or accessions. This functionality is designed to provide a fast and straightforward way to retrieve data without requiring advanced search criteria.

title: Home Page
text:
Access and explore options
The Home Page offers several options to access and explore metagenomic BGC data:
Explore BGCs gives access to more detailed searches using advanced criteria. This feature is particularly useful for users who need to perform complex queries or refine their search results based on specific parameters. Also provides basic statistics on the data portal alongside the detailed results of their searches.
API Access section provides documentation for users who wish to programmatically interact with the portal. Review the documentation, which includes endpoint descriptions and parameter details.
User Documentation section is where you will find guides on how to use the portal.
About section provides background information on the portal and the team behind its development.
This page is access point to exploring and utilising the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal.

title: Introduction
text:
Biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) encode the pathways for many natural products (for example antibiotics, siderophores, pigments and toxins). The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal gives natural product researchers rapid access to BGC predictions made uniformly across thousands of publicly available metagenomic assemblies processed by MGnify.
The site lets you search, filter, compare and download BGC‑related information, then (if you wish) move to programmatic access later. Multiple independent detection tools are combined so you can focus quickly on the strongest candidates.

title: Introduction
text:
Quick start (3 minutes)

Go to “Explore BGCs”.
Type a biome keyword (e.g. sediment) OR paste a BGC accession (MGYB…) OR select one or two BGC classes. Press Apply.
Sort a column (e.g. Classes) and switch to the UMAP tab to see class clusters. Click any point or table row to open full details.
Use the “Download results TSV” button if you want a local list to integrate with other tools.


Want to search by a Pfam domain combination or a chemical scaffold? Open the appropriate tab in the same form (Sequence or Chemical Structure) and submit—no need to leave the page.

title: Introduction
section: When to use each search mode
text:
When to use each search mode

Keyword (top bar): a quick way to jump to something you already know (accessions, simple terms).
Advanced Search: for combining biological, functional and structural criteria (class + Pfam + biome + completeness).
Sequence Search: when you have a nucleotide or amino‑acid sequence fragment and want to find similar predicted regions.
Chemical Structure Search: early exploratory link between a molecule (draw or SMILES) and clusters that may produce related chemotypes.
Sequence Analysis page: experimental embedding; paste a sequence to see nearest clusters as JSON output you can save for downstream work.

title: Introduction
text:
Basic navigation
The MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Portal is designed with three primary components that help users navigate and utilise its features effectively. Below is an overview of these components:

1. Home page
The Home Page is your entry point to the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters portal. Here, you can navigate to different sections of the site, perform a quick keyword search, and access key functionalities. The main features of the Home Page include:

Keyword search: Located at the top of the page, this allows you to search the portal using specific keywords or accession numbers, retrieving relevant records from both documentation and data. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/keyword_search/ endpoint of the API.
Access and explore options:

Search BGCs: Directs you to the Explore BGCs page for more detailed queries.
API Access: Provides documentation on how to use the API for programmatic access to BGC data.
User Documentation: Guides and examples to help you navigate and utilise the portal effectively.
Downloads: Access the FTP site to download the latest data.
About Us: Information about the project, including details on the EUREMAP initiative supporting marine bioprospecting research.




2. Explore BGCs page
The Explore BGCs page is where users can perform detailed searches and view the results. It includes:

Tabbed search interface: Three tabs (Advanced Search, Sequence Search, Chemical Structure Search) allow switching between search modalities without leaving the page.
Advanced Search panel: Build complex queries using BGC class names, accessions, biome lineage, Pfams, detector selection, and completeness. Equivalent to the GET /api/v1/advanced_search/ endpoint.
Sequence Search panel: Paste nucleotide or amino acid sequence data (FASTA / raw) to search for similar BGC regions using configurable similarity strategies.
Chemical Structure Search panel: Draw a compound using the embedded JSME editor or paste a SMILES string and search for BGCs associated with biosynthetic potential for structurally similar metabolites.
Results summary: High‑level counts (regions, studies, assemblies, class distribution) for the current query.
Results visualisation tabs: Table (sortable, paginated), Cards (compound‑oriented view including optional rendered structures), and UMAP (2‑D embedding with class colouring and MiBIG highlighting). Clicking a table row or UMAP point opens the BGC Details page in a new tab.
TSV export: Download current (optionally sorted) results for offline analysis.



3. BGC Details page
The BGC Details Page provides an in-depth view of a specific biosynthetic gene cluster. Key features include:

Aggregated region overview: Displays essential details such as assembly accession, contig information, biome data, and the BGC classes predicted.
BGC Plot: A visual representation of the BGC, including coding regions, Pfam annotations, and predictions from various detectors. Hovering and clicking elements display further information.
CDS Data: Detailed information about coding sequences, including protein sequences, Pfam descriptions, and links to external resources.
Download options: Allows you to download data related to the BGC in formats GeneBank, JSON, and FASTA. This is equivalent to calling the GET /api/v1/contig_region/ endpoint of the API using the MGYC and start/end positions of the aggregated region.

title: Introduction
text:
Use case examples
To help users better understand the practical applications of the platform, a series of use case notebooks have been provided. These notebooks demonstrate how to utilise the platform’s data and tools, including:
You can explore these use cases in detail through the Use Cases section of the portal.

title: Introduction
text:
Glossary

BGC (Biosynthetic Gene Cluster): A stretch of DNA containing the genes that together can make a natural product.
MGYB: The stable identifier we assign to an (aggregated) predicted region (the “BGC accession”). Use it to find or cite that region.
MGYC: A contig accession—the sequence fragment (assembly piece) on which the BGC sits.
Completeness (Full / Single / Double): Whether the cluster appears fully internal to the contig (Full) or cut off by one (Single) or both (Double) contig ends (likely partial).
Pfam: A protein domain family; filtering by Pfam helps target clusters encoding specific enzymatic components.
UMAP: A method to project high‑dimensional similarity between clusters into 2D so you can visually see grouping by class or novelty.
Asynchronous search: You submit a query; the server works in the background; the page auto‑updates when ready.
SMILES: A compact text way to describe a chemical structure (can be pasted or generated by the drawing tool).
MiBIG: A reference collection of experimentally characterised biosynthetic gene clusters; used for the highlighted points in some plots.

For more detailed instructions, please refer to the specific sections of the documentation.

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
In this notebook, we will explore how to use the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters (BGCs) portal and its API to discover Ribosomally Synthesised and Post-translationally Modified Peptides (RiPPs) that contain glycosyltransferase domains and are associated with toxin-antitoxin systems.
RiPPs are a diverse class of natural products that begin as simple peptides synthesised by the ribosome. These peptides undergo various post-translational modifications, resulting in complex structures with unique biological activities, including antimicrobial, antiviral, and enzyme-inhibiting properties.
Adding sugar moieties through glycosyltransferases can result in enhanced solubility, stability, and bioactivity of RiPPs, making them more suitable for therapeutic applications. Additionally, some RiPPs are associated with toxin-antitoxin (TA) systems, where the peptide acts as a potent toxin neutralised by an antitoxin in the producing organism.
These features make RiPPs with both glycosyl modifications and TA systems promising in biotechnology for developing new antibiotics, cancer therapies, biopesticides, and more.



Image





Use the MGnify BGCs API to find RiPPs that:

Contain glycosyltransferase domains.

Download and analyse the genomic regions of these BGCs.

Find RiPPs associated with antitoxin systems.

Visualise genomic context to gain further insights into these BGCs.

Explore connected resources for further considerations and downstream analyses.

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
In this notebook, we will explore how to use the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters (BGCs) portal and its API to discover Ribosomally Synthesised and Post-translationally Modified Peptides (RiPPs) that contain glycosyltransferase domains and are associated with toxin-antitoxin systems.
RiPPs are a diverse class of natural products that begin as simple peptides synthesised by the ribosome. These peptides undergo various post-translational modifications, resulting in complex structures with unique biological activities, including antimicrobial, antiviral, and enzyme-inhibiting properties.
Adding sugar moieties through glycosyltransferases can result in enhanced solubility, stability, and bioactivity of RiPPs, making them more suitable for therapeutic applications. Additionally, some RiPPs are associated with toxin-antitoxin (TA) systems, where the peptide acts as a potent toxin neutralised by an antitoxin in the producing organism.
These features make RiPPs with both glycosyl modifications and TA systems promising in biotechnology for developing new antibiotics, cancer therapies, biopesticides, and more.



Image





Use the MGnify BGCs API to find RiPPs that:

Contain glycosyltransferase domains.

Download and analyse the genomic regions of these BGCs.

Find RiPPs associated with antitoxin systems.

Visualise genomic context to gain further insights into these BGCs.

Explore connected resources for further considerations and downstream analyses.

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
Step 1: Import necessary libraries
First, we install and import the required libraries.

!pip install tqdm biopython


import requests
import json
from tqdm import tqdm
import os
from Bio import SeqIO

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
Step 2: Identify glycosyltransferase Pfam domains
We need to obtain Pfam accession numbers for glycosyltransferase domains. Pfam is a database of protein families represented by multiple sequence alignments and hidden Markov models (HMMs). Pfam is widley used for functional annotation of genes.

Visit the InterPro entry for glycosyltransferases.
Download the table of Pfam entries related to glycosyltransferases.
Extract the Pfam accession numbers.

For this example, we’ll use a predefined list of glycosyltransferase Pfam accessions.

# List of Pfam accessions for glycosyltransferases
glycosyltransferase_pfams = [
    'PF00777', 'PF00852', 'PF00982', 'PF01075', 'PF01697', 'PF01755', 'PF03033', 'PF03155',
    'PF03360', 'PF03385', 'PF03414', 'PF04101', 'PF04488', 'PF04572', 'PF04577', 'PF04724',
    'PF04765', 'PF06165', 'PF07922', 'PF08759', 'PF09594', 'PF10111', 'PF11397', 'PF12919',
    'PF13439', 'PF13641', 'PF13712', 'PF14307', 'PF15024', 'PF16849', 'PF18674', 'PF20156',
    'PF20157', 'PF20471', 'PF20691', 'PF20706', 'PF21129', 'PF21912'
    ]
print(f"Number of glycosyltransferase Pfam domains: {len(glycosyltransferase_pfams)}")

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
section: Step 3: Construct API queries to search for RiPPs with glycosyltransferase domains
text:
Step 3: Construct API queries to search for RiPPs with glycosyltransferase domains
We will use the MGnify BGCs API to search for BGCs that:

Are classified as RiPPs.
Contain one or more glycosyltransferase Pfam domains.
Detected by at least one tool (antiSMASH, GECCO, SanntiS).

We define a function to build the API URL with the required parameters.


# Base URL for the advanced search API
base_api_url = "http://ebi.ac.uk/finn-srv/mgnify-bgcs/api/v1/advanced_search/"

# Parameters for the API query
bgc_class = 'RiPP'
detectors = ['antiSMASH', 'GECCO', 'SanntiS']
pfam_strategy = 'union'
aggregate_strategy = 'single'
page_limit = 100  # Number of results per page

# Function to build the API URL for a given Pfam accession
def build_api_url(pfam_accession, offset=0):
    params = {
        'bgc_class_name': bgc_class,
        'completeness': [0, 1, 2],
        'protein_pfam': pfam_accession,
        'pfam_strategy': pfam_strategy,
        'detectors': detectors,
        'aggregate_strategy': aggregate_strategy,
        'limit': page_limit,
        'offset': offset
    }
    # Build the query string
    query_string = '&'.join([f"{key}={value}" if not isinstance(value, list) else '&'.join([f"{key}={v}" for v in value]) for key, value in params.items()])
    return f"{base_api_url}?{query_string}"

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
section: Step 4: Fetch BGCs matching the criteria
text:
Step 4: Fetch BGCs matching the criteria
We loop through each glycosyltransferase Pfam accession and retrieve all matching BGCs.


# List to store all BGC entries
all_bgcs = []

# Loop through each Pfam accession
for pfam_acc in tqdm(glycosyltransferase_pfams, desc="Processing Pfam domains"):
    offset = 0
    total_results = 1  # Initialise with a value greater than 0
    while offset < total_results:
        # Build the API URL
        api_url = build_api_url(pfam_acc, offset=offset)
        # Make the API request
        response = requests.get(api_url)
        if response.status_code == 200:
            data = response.json()
            total_results = data['count']
            all_bgcs.extend(data['items'])
            offset += page_limit
        else:
            print(f"Failed to fetch data for Pfam {pfam_acc}. Status code: {response.status_code}")
            break  # Exit the loop for this Pfam accession
print(f"BGC prediction found with at least one glycosyltransferase domain: {len(all_bgcs)}")

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
section: Step 5: Download GenBank files of the BGCs using the portal’s API
text:
Step 5: Download GenBank files of the BGCs using the portal’s API
We download the GenBank files of the BGC regions for further analysis.

# Directory to save the GBK files
output_dir = "bgc_gbk_files"
os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

# URL template for fetching the GBK files
gbk_url_template = "http://ebi.ac.uk/finn-srv/mgnify-bgcs/api/v1/contig_region/?mgyc={}&start_position={}&end_position={}&output_type=gbk"

# Loop through all BGC entries and download the GBK files
for bgc in tqdm(all_bgcs, desc="Downloading GBK files"):
    mgyc = bgc['contig_mgyc']
    start = bgc['start_position']
    end = bgc['end_position']

    # Construct the GBK URL
    gbk_url = gbk_url_template.format(mgyc, start, end)

    # File path to save the GBK file
    filename = f"{mgyc}_{start}_{end}.gbk"
    filepath = os.path.join(output_dir, filename)

    # Check if the file already exists
    if not os.path.isfile(filepath):
        # Send request to fetch GBK file
        response = requests.get(gbk_url)
        if response.status_code == 200:
            with open(filepath, 'wb') as file:
                file.write(response.content)
        else:
            print(f"Failed to fetch GBK file for {mgyc}. Status code: {response.status_code}")

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
section: Step 6: Identify BGCs associated with Toxin-Antitoxin systems
text:
Step 6: Identify BGCs associated with Toxin-Antitoxin systems
We parse the downloaded GBK files to search for annotations containing ‘antitoxin’.


# List to store BGCs associated with antitoxin systems
antitoxin_regions = []

# Loop through the GBK files and parse annotations
for bgc in tqdm(all_bgcs, desc="Parsing GBK files for antitoxin annotations"):
    mgyc = bgc['contig_mgyc']
    start = bgc['start_position']
    end = bgc['end_position']

    # File path to the GBK file
    filename = f"{mgyc}_{start}_{end}.gbk"
    filepath = os.path.join(output_dir, filename)

    try:

        # Parse the GBK file
        with open(filepath, 'r') as gbk_file:
            record = SeqIO.read(gbk_file, "genbank")

        # Flag to indicate if antitoxin annotation is found
        FLAG =False
        glycos = []
        antitoxin_pfam = []

        # Iterate through features
        for feature in record.features:
            if feature.type == "ANNOT":
                # Check if the feature has a 'description' qualifier
                if 'description' in feature.qualifiers:
                    description = feature.qualifiers['description'][0]  # Get the description text
                    ID = feature.qualifiers['ID'][0]  # Get the description text

                    # Check if 'antitoxin' is present in the description (case-insensitive)
                    if 'antitoxin' in description.lower():
                        # If found, save the mgyc, start, and end values
                        FLAG=True
                        antitoxin_pfam.append(ID)
                    if ID in glycosyltransferase_pfams:
                         glycos.append(ID)
        if FLAG:
            antitoxin_regions.append((mgyc, start, end,antitoxin_pfam,glycos))
            FLAG =False

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error parsing GBK file {filename}: {e}")

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
section: Step 7: Visualise the found RiPPs using the BGC Portal
text:
Step 7: Visualise the found RiPPs using the BGC Portal
Now that we have identified BGCs that are RiPPs, contain glycosyltransferase domains, and are associated with antitoxin systems, we can explore these BGCs further using the MGnify BGCs portal.
For each BGC, we will:

Construct the URL to the BGC’s page on the MGnify BGCs portal.
Explore the genomic context and annotations.
Examine the specific CDS related to the toxin-antitoxin systems.
View the representative protein pages to see protein structures and distribution across biomes.
Access the sample information in MGnify to learn about the sample’s origin, geographic location, and ABS (Access and Benefit Sharing) compliance considerations.



# Iterate over the antitoxin_regions
for region in antitoxin_regions:
    mgyc = region[0]
    start = region[1]
    end = region[2]
    antitoxin_pfams = region[3]
    glycosyltransferase_pfams_in_bgc = region[4]

    # Construct the URL to the BGC portal page for this BGC
    bgc_url = f"http://ebi.ac.uk/finn-srv/mgnify-bgcs/bgc/{mgyc}/{start}/{end}/"

    print(f"BGC URL: {bgc_url}")
    print(f"Antitoxin Pfam Domains: {antitoxin_pfams}")
    print(f"Glycosyltransferase Pfam Domains in BGC: {glycosyltransferase_pfams_in_bgc}")
    print("Visit this URL to explore the BGC in detail.")
    print("---")

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
Step 8: Summary and conclusions
In this notebook, we:

Used the MGnify BGCs API to search for RiPP BGCs containing glycosyltransferase domains.
Downloaded and parsed GenBank files to identify BGCs associated with antitoxin systems.
Visualised and analysed results using the MGnify BGCs portal to have a better understanding of result.
Explored connected resources, improved contextualisation of the BGCs by exploring sample metadata and links from other resources for further analysis.

This workflow demonstrates how to integrate MGnify BGCs portal to other bioinformatics tools and databases to accelerate natural product discovery.

Next Steps:

Analyse protein sequences using tools like BLAST or InterProScan.
Predict biosynthetic pathways and potential structures of the natural products.
Assess legal and ethical considerations, including ABS obligations.

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
References

MGnify BGCs Portal: http://ebi.ac.uk/finn-srv/mgnify-bgcs/
MGnify API Documentation: http://ebi.ac.uk/finn-srv/mgnify-bgcs/api/v1/docs/
MGnify: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/metagenomics/
MGnify Proteins: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/metagenomics/proteins/
Interpro: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/

title: Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems using the MGnify BGCs Portal
text:
Conclusion
By following this notebook, you’ve learned how to leverage the MGnify BGCs portal and API for targeted natural product discovery. This approach can be adapted to search for other classes of BGCs or functional domains, facilitating the exploration of the vast diversity of microbial natural products.

title: Use Cases
text: The following use cases demonstrate how to explore and analyse biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) using the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters (BGCs) Portal. Each use case guides users through practical examples of how to integrate the platform’s features with other resources, including using the API, performing advanced searches, visualising results, and downloading relevant datasets for further analysis.

title: Use Cases
text:
Available use cases

1. Exploring RiPPs with Glycosyltransferase domains and Toxin-Antitoxin systems
In this notebook, we explore how to use the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters (BGCs) portal and its API to discover Ribosomally Synthesised and Post-translationally Modified Peptides (RiPPs) containing glycosyltransferase domains that are associated with toxin-antitoxin systems.
This notebook covers: - How to use the MGnify API to find RiPPs with glycosyltransferase domains. - Steps to download and analyse genomic regions of interest. - Visualising the genomic context to gain further insights into the data.

title: API Documentation
text: For detailed information on how to use the API, including available endpoints and request formats, please refer to the comprehensive MGnify BGCs API documentation available.

title: API Documentation
text: For detailed information on how to use the API, including available endpoints and request formats, please refer to the comprehensive MGnify BGCs API documentation available.

title: API Documentation
text:
What is an API?
An API (Application Programming Interface) allows different software applications to communicate with each other. In the context of the MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Portal, the API provides a programmatic way for users to access and interact with the portal’s data. This can be especially useful for automating workflows, integrating the portal’s data with other bioinformatics tools, or performing large-scale analyses without needing to use the web interface.

title: About
text:
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is a portal for exploring predicted biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) across MGnify metagenomic datasets. It standardises the output of several widely used detection tools (antiSMASH, GECCO, SanntiS) so you can quickly compare and prioritise candidate regions without setting up complex software. Non‑technical users can work entirely through the web interface; programmatic users can extend their analysis via the API.
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters aims to make this valuable dataset widely accessible, promoting advances in natural product science across research, industry, and the broader community. The portal is part of MGnify, which offers automated pipelines for the analysis and archiving of microbiome data. MGnify helps determine the taxonomic diversity and functional and metabolic potential of environmental samples. Users can submit their data for analysis or browse the publicly available datasets within the repository. Additionally, MGnify can analyse datasets from the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) upon request.

title: About
text:
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is a portal for exploring predicted biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) across MGnify metagenomic datasets. It standardises the output of several widely used detection tools (antiSMASH, GECCO, SanntiS) so you can quickly compare and prioritise candidate regions without setting up complex software. Non‑technical users can work entirely through the web interface; programmatic users can extend their analysis via the API.
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters aims to make this valuable dataset widely accessible, promoting advances in natural product science across research, industry, and the broader community. The portal is part of MGnify, which offers automated pipelines for the analysis and archiving of microbiome data. MGnify helps determine the taxonomic diversity and functional and metabolic potential of environmental samples. Users can submit their data for analysis or browse the publicly available datasets within the repository. Additionally, MGnify can analyse datasets from the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) upon request.

title: About
text:
Dataset overview
The underlying dataset collects BGC predictions from MGnify metagenomic assemblies using antiSMASH [1], GECCO [2] and SanntiS [3]. Outputs are harmonised and stored with links to contigs, assemblies and protein annotations. The open pipeline describes the processing steps.

title: About
text:
Funding and support
This portal has been developed at EMBL-EBI by the MGnify team as part of the EUREMAP project. EUREMAP is a European initiative designed to create an integrated pipeline of marine bioprospecting services to discover novel bioactive natural products from marine organisms. This project connects research infrastructures, industry, and academia strengthening the blue economy.
EUREMAP project is funded by the European Union under grant agreement No. 101131663 (HORIZON-INFRA-2023-DEV-01-04). Views and opinions expressed are however those of the author(s) only and do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the Agency. Neither the European Union nor the granting authority can be held responsible for them.

title: About
text:
Contact
MGnify Biosynthetic Gene Clusters is currently in beta and under active development. We value feedback and encourage users to share any issues or suggestions via our contact form.

title: About
text:
References
[1] Blin, Kai, Simon Shaw, Hannah E. Augustijn, Zachary L. Reitz, Friederike Biermann, Mohammad Alanjary, Artem Fetter et al. “antiSMASH 7.0: new and improved predictions for detection, regulation, chemical structures and visualisation.” Nucleic acids research 51, no. W1 (2023): W46-W50.
[2] Carroll, Laura M., Martin Larralde, Jonas Simon Fleck, Ruby Ponnudurai, Alessio Milanese, Elisa Cappio, and Georg Zeller. “Accurate de novo identification of biosynthetic gene clusters with GECCO.” BioRxiv (2021): 2021-05.
[3] Sanchez, Santiago, Joel D. Rogers, Alexander B. Rogers, Maaly Nassar, Johanna McEntyre, Martin Welch, Florian Hollfelder, and Robert D. Finn. “Expansion of novel biosynthetic gene clusters from diverse environments using SanntiS.” bioRxiv (2023): 2023-05.

title: BGC Details
text: This page shows metadata about a selected predicted biosynthetic region: context, genes, functional domains, detector support and downloads.

title: BGC Details
text:
Overview section
At the top of the BGC details page, users will find summary information and related datasets about the aggregated region. This includes:


Sample information

Assembly accession: The ENA (European Nucleotide Archive) assembly accession associated with the predicted BGC region. This is a unique identifier for the assembly from which the BGC was predicted. The assembly accession is linked to the MGnify assembly page, where users can explore the assembly in more detail.
Biome: Information about the biome from which the assembly originates. Biome data provides context on the environmental source of the DNA sequence, which can be important for understanding the ecological relevance of the BGC.
Contig information: This includes contig accession (MGYC) where the BGC was predicted, as well as the start and end positions of the aggregated region.
Predicted classes: Each detector’s class call—agreement increases confidence.
Functional annotation: Slimmed GO functional hints for enzymes in the region; scan for tailoring/modification clues.

title: BGC Details
text:
Explore region

Region plot
The plot is a compact interactive map of the region:

– CDS (genes): White arrows (direction = strand). Click to see protein sequence & domains. – Pfam domains: Coloured mini‑arrows inside genes; colour reflects general functional category. – Tool predictions: Coloured horizontal bars—compare extent and overlap.
Hovering the cursor over the elements will display accession and other information.


Protein information

Clicking a gene reveals:

Protein sequence: Copy for alignment or motif checks.
Pfam details: InterPro‑linked descriptions—use to infer enzymatic steps.
Representative protein: Link to MGnify protein cluster representative for broader context.

title: BGC Details
text:
Download options
The BGC details page offers download options for users who wish to retrieve data for further analysis:

GeneBank: This file contains the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region, along with annotated features such as predicted genes, translation sequences, Pfam domains, and BGC region information. The GeneBank format is widely used for storing detailed sequence data, and it is compatible with various bioinformatics tools.
JSON: The JSON file is formatted according to antiSMASH specifications and can be used directly within the antiSMASH ecosystem. This file is useful for users who want to perform further analysis using antiSMASH tools or integrate the data into their existing antiSMASH workflows.
FASTA: This file provides the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region in FASTA format. FASTA is a simple, widely-used format for representing nucleotide sequences, making it easy to use with various sequence analysis tools.


Choose the format that fits your next step—GenBank for genome browsers, JSON (antiSMASH‑style) for re‑analysis, FASTA for sequence Alignments.

title: BGC Details
text:
Quick interpretation tips

Consistent class across detectors = stronger lead.
Many tailoring/modification domains (e.g. glycosyltransferase, methyltransferase) can point to structural novelty.
“Double” completeness in search results usually means partial edges—treat with caution for cloning or heterologous expression planning.
Compare span differences between tools: wide disagreement may indicate fragmented assembly or borderline confidence.
